findings.mechanism: `create = handle(async (req, res) => { const plan = await NursingCarePlan.create(req.body); ... })` spreads the entire client body into the model with no allow-list. NursingCarePlanModel.js:123-135 declares exactly the fields that must be server-derived: `status` (enum includes "completed"), `signedBy` (ref User), `signedAt`, `createdBy`, `updatedBy`, `completedAt`. The PUT handler in the same file (lines 65-68) explicitly does `delete body.signedBy; delete body.signedAt;` with the comment "Don't let callers backdoor signedBy / signedAt via PUT" — proving the intent — but the POST path has no equivalent guard, so the exact backdoor the PUT closes is wide open one route above it (route gate is the same `mar.write`, nursingCarePlanRoutes.js:19 vs :21). Worse, the pre-save immutability hook (NursingCarePlanModel.js:151-165) then makes the forgery permanent.
findings.failureScenario: Nurse B (role Nurse, has mar.write) POSTs `{patient:"<oid>", UHID:"UH-2026-000123", ipdNo:"IPD-2026-0007", signedBy:"<Nurse A's User _id>", signedAt:"2026-01-01T00:00:00Z", status:"completed", completedAt:"2026-01-01T00:00:00Z", createdBy:"<Nurse A's NurseStaff _id>", nursingProblems:[...]}`. A care plan is persisted that a NABH surveyor reads as authored, completed and signed by Nurse A on a date Nurse A never touched it. Nurse A cannot repudiate it and cannot correct it: PUT returns 409 CARE_PLAN_LOCKED (controller lines 58-64) and the pre-save hook rejects any non-"amended" mutation. Non-repudiation for the signed clinical record is destroyed.
findings.fixSketch: Mirror the PUT guard on create: build an explicit allow-list (patient, UHID, patientName, age, gender, admissionId, ipdNo, primaryNurse, nurseName, attendingDoctor, department, assessmentDate, admissionAssessment, nursingProblems, education*, dischargeGoals, expectedDischargeDate, reviewDate) and force `status = "draft"`, `signedBy = null`, `signedAt = null`, `completedAt = null`, `createdBy = req.user.id` server-side. Signing should only be reachable via a dedicated sign endpoint that stamps req.user.
findings.verifyEvidence: Every cited location matches the real code; adversarial checks strengthened the finding. MECHANISM VERIFIED EXACTLY AS FILED: 1. Backend/controllers/Nurse/nursingCarePlanController.js:15-18 — the create handler is verbatim `create = handle(async (req, res) => { const plan = await NursingCarePlan.create(req.body); return res.status(201).json({success:true,data:plan}); })`. Three lines, no allow-list, no req.user stamping. 2. Same file:65-68 — the PUT guard exists exactly as quoted, including the comment "Don't let callers backdoor signedBy / signedAt via PUT" followed by `delete body.signedBy; delete body.signedAt;`. Intent is proven in-file; POST has no equivalent. 3. Backend/routes/Nurse/nursingCarePlanRoutes.js:19 (POST) and :21 (PUT) are both `requireAction("mar.write")` — identical gate, asymmetric protection. 4. Backend/config/permissions.js:134 — `"mar.write": ["Admin", "Nurse"]`. Any Nurse reaches the unguarded POST. 5. Backend/models/Nurse/NursingCarePlanModel.js:123-135 — `status` enum genuinely contains "completed"; `signedBy` (ref User), `signedAt`, `completedAt`, `createdBy`, `updatedBy` are all declared schema paths, so Mongoose strict mode ACCEPTS them from the body rather than dropping them. 6. Same model:151-165 — pre-save hook throws on any non-"amended" mutation once `_priorStatus` is completed/COMPLETED. ADVERSARIAL CHECKS THAT FAILED TO REFUTE: a) No global body sanitizer. Backend/index.js has only express.json (115), cors, rate limiters, and a cache-cont…

findings.dimension: Data security — PHI exposure, injection, upload, IDOR/BOLA
findings.title: GET /api/patients?limit=999999 — client-controlled, uncapped page size dumps the entire patient PHI collection (the hard cap added to /search was never applied to the list twin)
findings.mechanism: patientController.js:57 passes `req.query` verbatim: `patientService.getAllPatients(req.query)`. patientService.js:187 destructures `limit = 1000` — a DEFAULT, not a ceiling — and line 226 does `.limit(parseInt(limit))` with no clamp. The sibling `searchPatients` at patientService.js:244 was explicitly hardened (`const lim = Math.min(parseInt(limit) || 50, 50); // R7bf-J/A8-CRIT-1: hard cap`), so the ceiling exists in the codebase but was never applied to the list endpoint. The query also `.populate()`s three refs (lines 222-224) and returns non-lean hydrated docs, multiplying the cost per row. The route gate (patientRoutes.js:33) is `patient.read`, which per the file's own comment spans ~9 roles including Lab Tech / Pharmacist / TPA / Accountant.
findings.failureScenario: Any account holding `patient.read` — e.g. a shared Lab Technician login, or a stolen Pharmacist token — issues one request: `GET /api/patients?limit=500000`. On a 30k-patient hospital DB the backend hydrates and populates every Patient document and serialises full demographics (name, contactNumber, email, DOB, address, policyNumber, allergies) into a single JSON response: a complete PHI/PII exfiltration in one call, plus a multi-hundred-MB heap spike that can OOM the Node process. Secondary: `?page=0` makes `skip = -parseInt(limit)`, a negative skip that Mongo rejects → 500.
findings.fixSketch: In patientService.getAllPatients apply the same clamp as searchPatients: `const lim = Math.min(Math.max(parseInt(limit) || 50, 1), 100)` and `const pg = Math.max(parseInt(page) || 1, 1)`; use `.lean()`. Consider a shared `parsePaging(req.query)` helper in utils/queryGuards.js so no future list endpoint re-introduces the gap.
findings.verifyEvidence: Every cited leg of the mechanism is present in the code as described; I attempted to refute it three ways (a clamping middleware, a shared paging guard, a field-level projection) and found none. 1) Client-controlled limit reaches Mongo unclamped — CONFIRMED.    - `controllers/Patient/patientController.js:57`: `const result = await patientService.getAllPatients(req.query);` — `req.query` passed verbatim, no whitelist/normalisation (contrast with `createPatient` at :27, which DOES normalise via `_normaliseRegistrationType`).    - `services/Patient/patientService.js:187`: `limit = 1000,` is a destructuring DEFAULT (applies only when the key is absent), not a ceiling.    - `patientService.js:226`: `.limit(parseInt(limit))` — no `Math.min`, no upper bound. `?limit=500000` flows straight through. 2) The ceiling exists in the same file but only on the sibling — CONFIRMED verbatim.    - `patientService.js:244`: `const lim = Math.min(parseInt(limit) || 50, 50); // R7bf-J/A8-CRIT-1: hard cap` inside `searchPatients`, applied at `.limit(lim)` (:271). The list twin never got it. The asymmetry is real. 3) No compensating control anywhere.    - Grepped all of `Backend/` for `parsePaging|queryGuards|clampLimit|MAX_PAGE_SIZE|sanitizeQuery`: `utils/queryGuards.js` exports only `safeRegex`, `escapeRegex`, `validateObjectIdParam`, and IST/date helpers — there is NO paging guard in the codebase, so no middleware clamps this before the service.    - `routes/Patient/patientRoutes.js:33`: `router.…

findings.dimension: Data security — PHI exposure, injection, upload, IDOR/BOLA
findings.title: Error logger and ClientErrorLog persist the request URL unredacted — UHIDs travel in the path, so PHI identifiers land in flat log files and an Admin-readable Mongo collection
findings.mechanism: errorLogger.js:184-186 carefully runs `redactPHI` over body/query/params, and PHI_KEYS (line 97) explicitly includes `"UHID", "uhid", "ipdNo", "opdNo", "admissionNumber", "billNumber"`; errorLogger.js:179-181 additionally masks emails and `\b\d{6,}\b` runs out of errorMessage (per R7hr-241). But line 170 writes `route: req?.originalUrl` with NO redaction and NO masking — and this codebase routes PHI identifiers through the PATH, not the body: `/api/patient-file/:uhid/complete`, `/api/patient-history/:uhid/opd`, `/api/patients/uhid/:uhid`, `/api/nursing-care-plans/uhid/:uhid`, `/api/opd/uhid/:UHID/today-rx`. The identical gap exists in clientErrorRoutes.js:115: `url: clamp(typeof body.url === "string" ? body.url : "", 500)` — clamped but never passed through `sanitiseString` (line 57-67), unlike message/stack/componentStack on lines 111-113 which are. That value is persisted to the ClientErrorLog collection and re-served verbatim by GET /api/client-errors (line 148-152) and the `lastUrl` aggregation (line 173).
findings.failureScenario: A Nurse opens the complete file for UH-2026-000123 and the aggregator throws (e.g. a CastError on a malformed sub-document). errorLogger writes `{"route":"/api/patient-file/UH-2026-000123/complete", ...}` to Backend/logs/errors-2026-07-17.log while diligently redacting the empty GET body beside it. Separately, a React ErrorBoundary on /patient-history-view/UH-2026-000123 POSTs `{message:"...", url:"https://his.hospital/patient-history-view/UH-2026-000123"}` anonymously to /api/client-errors; the message is PHI-scrubbed, the url is stored raw. Result: a DPDP §8-relevant identifier set accumulates in a plaintext file any ops engineer can `tail` with no need-to-know, and in a Mongo collection — defeating the redaction control that the surrounding code implements correctly.
findings.fixSketch: In errorLogger.js build the route through a path scrubber before assigning: replace UHID-shaped and number-shaped path segments, e.g. `String(req.originalUrl||"").replace(/\b(UH|SH)[-\w]*\d{3,}\b/gi,"[uhid]").replace(/\b\d{6,}\b/g,"[num]")`, or prefer `req.route?.path` (the un-substituted pattern) plus the already-redacted `requestParams`. In clientErrorRoutes.js:115 route `body.url` through `sanitiseString(...)` like message/stack already are.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM CONFIRMED VERBATIM AT BOTH SITES; IMPACT OVERSTATED. (1) errorLogger.js:170 is exactly `route: req?.originalUrl || req?.url || null` — no redaction, no masking. The contrast is real: :179-182 masks errorMessage (`/[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+/g`→"[email]", `/\b\d{6,}\b/g`→"[num]"), and :184-186 runs redactPHI over body/query/params. PHI_KEYS (line 97) does include "UHID","uhid","ipdNo","opdNo","admissionNumber","billNumber", so requestParams.uhid becomes "[REDACTED]" on the same line where `route` prints it in cleartext. Genuine self-defeating inconsistency. (2) PHI-in-path confirmed: patientFileRoutes.js:19 `/:uhid/complete`, patientHistoryRoutes.js:38 `/:uhid/opd`, patientRoutes.js:27 `/uhid/:uhid`, marRoutes.js:21, billingRoutes.js:93-116, ~40 more. Middleware is live: index.js:1412 `app.use(require("./middleware/errorLogger"))`. (3) clientErrorRoutes.js:115 confirmed: `url: clamp(typeof body.url === "string" ? body.url : "", 500)` — clamped, never through sanitiseString (:57-67), unlike message/stack/componentStack at :111-113. Route IS mounted (routes/index.js:97, anonymous-reachable via attemptAuth + clientErrorRateLimit). Frontend ErrorBoundary.jsx:51 sends `window.location.href` raw. Persisted then re-served at :148-152 and as `lastUrl` at :176. Frontend routes do carry uhid (App.jsx:1037 `/patient-history-view/:uhid`, :472 `/patient-file/:uhid`). DOWNGRADE RATIONALE — the failure scenario's identifier is fabricated. patientModel.js:277-281 is authoritative: "/…

findings.dimension: Data security — PHI exposure, injection, upload, IDOR/BOLA
findings.title: controllers/Doctorcontroller.js is a dead, unloadable file (starts with a stray `n;`) that also contains the codebase's only unescaped user-input RegExp
findings.mechanism: The file's first bytes are literally `n;\r\n` (verified: raw bytes 110,59,13,10) — a bare identifier reference that throws `ReferenceError: n is not defined` the instant the module is required. It is required by nothing: a repo-wide grep for "Doctorcontroller" returns only a stale SECURITY_FINDINGS.md row (line 139) claiming G-03 was FIXED here. So the file is unreachable dead code, and the audit trail points at a file no route can reach. It also carries two patterns the rest of the codebase has systematically eliminated: line 43 `Doctor.findOne({ UHID: new RegExp(`^${UHID}$`, "i") })` — the ONLY user-input RegExp in Backend/ with no `escapeRegex`/`replace(/[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g,...)` call (every other of ~60 call sites escapes; utils/queryGuards.js:16 exists for exactly this) — and line 7 `new Doctor(req.body)` unfiltered mass-assignment.
findings.failureScenario: Two ways this bites. (a) Maintenance trap: anyone who wires this controller into a route — the obvious next step, since `getdoctorPatientbyID` looks complete — instantly crashes boot on the `n;`, and once that one-char typo is fixed they ship a regex-injection/ReDoS endpoint: `GET /.../((a+)+)+$` pins the event loop, and `GET /.../.*` returns an arbitrary patient record instead of a UHID-exact match. (b) Audit integrity: SECURITY_FINDINGS.md G-03 records a remediation in a file that cannot execute, so the finding register overstates coverage.
findings.fixSketch: Delete Backend/controllers/Doctorcontroller.js and retire the SECURITY_FINDINGS.md G-03 row (or re-point it at the live controller). If the lookup is genuinely wanted, re-implement it on the existing Doctor router using `escapeRegex` from utils/queryGuards.js and an explicit field allow-list instead of `new Doctor(req.body)`.
findings.verifyEvidence: All four mechanical claims verified against the actual code; two empirically. 1. STRAY `n;` — CONFIRMED empirically, not just by reading. Backend/controllers/Doctorcontroller.js first bytes are [110,59,13,10] = "n;\r\n". Executing `require('./controllers/Doctorcontroller.js')` under node yields: `ReferenceError - n is not defined`. The module provably cannot load. 2. UNREACHABLE DEAD CODE — CONFIRMED. Repo-wide grep for "Doctorcontroller" returns exactly one hit outside the file: SECURITY_FINDINGS.md:139. The live controller is a DIFFERENT file, controllers/Doctor/doctorController.js, which is what routes/Doctor/doctorRoutes.js:3 requires and mounts (getAllDoctors, searchDoctors, getDoctorById, etc.). Checked for dynamic loading (readdirSync / require(path.join(...)) over controllers/) — only hits are in scripts/, targeting models/services. No route or module reaches this file. ADDITIONAL EVIDENCE THE AUDITOR MISSED (corroborates orphan status): Frontend/src/Services/userService.js:86-90 defines getdoctorpatientbyID calling `GET ${API_BASE_URL}/doctordetail/doctoradd/${UHID}`. Grep of Backend for "doctordetail"/"doctoradd" = zero route matches. That frontend call is a guaranteed 404 — the controller was orphaned (route deleted), not merely never-wired. 3. LINE 43 UNESCAPED REGEX, UNIQUE IN Backend/ — CONFIRMED. `UHID: new RegExp(`^${UHID}$`, "i")` interpolates req.params.UHID raw. Grep of the anchored-template pattern across Backend returns 9 sites: 7 escape (abdmLinkService…

findings.dimension: Concurrency & data integrity — races, TOCTOU, counters
findings.title: Non-transactional admission rollback frees a bed that belongs to a DIFFERENT patient (unconditional compensation after a lost CAS)
findings.mechanism: createAdmission claims the bed with a correct atomic CAS: `Bed.findOneAndUpdate({_id, status:"Available", housekeeping.state:{$nin:[...]}} , {$set:{status:"Occupied"}})` (L149-157). If the CAS loses (bed already Occupied by someone else), `bed` is null and the code throws `Bed X is not available (current status: Occupied)` (L159-165). On the no-transaction path that throw is caught at L305 and the compensation at L309 fires on `if (data.bedId)` ALONE — it never checks whether *this* invocation actually won the CAS. It then unconditionally executes `Bed.findByIdAndUpdate(data.bedId, {$set:{status:"Available", currentAdmission:null}})`, releasing a bed this request never owned and destroying the incumbent admission's `currentAdmission` back-link. `useTx` is only true when mongoose detects `replicaSet` (L142-144), so any single-node Mongo deployment (the per-hospital Docker install) runs this path in production.
findings.failureScenario: Patient A is admitted to bed B-12 (status=Occupied, currentAdmission=admA). Receptionist 2 works from a stale bed list and POSTs an admission for Patient X with bedId=B-12. The CAS at L149 matches nothing (status is Occupied, not Available) → bed=null → throw at L162. The catch at L305 sees data.bedId is set and runs the L310 rollback: B-12 becomes status="Available", currentAdmission=null — while Patient A is still physically in it. The bed now reappears in getAvailableBeds; the next admission's CAS legitimately succeeds and Patient X is assigned to A's occupied bed (real double-booking, patient-safety event). A's bed-day charge accrual, which keys on bed.currentAdmission, is simultaneously orphaned so IPD room rent silently stops billing.
findings.fixSketch: Track ownership explicitly: `let bedClaimed = false;` set to true only immediately after a non-null CAS result inside run(); gate the rollback on `if (data.bedId && bedClaimed)`. Better, scope the compensation to the row this call wrote: `Bed.findOneAndUpdate({_id: data.bedId, currentAdmission: created?._id ?? null, status:"Occupied"}, {$set:{status:"Available", currentAdmission:null}})` so it is a no-op when another admission owns the bed. Same audit should be applied to the mirrored pattern in services/MLC/mlcService.js.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM FULLY CONFIRMED at the cited lines. admissionService.js:149-157 claims the bed with a correct CAS ({_id, status:"Available", housekeeping.state:{$nin:["CleaningPending","CleaningInProgress"]}} -> {$set:{status:"Occupied"}}). :159-165 throws when the CAS returns null. :303-315 catches that throw and the compensation at :309 is gated on `if (data.bedId)` ALONE with no ownership check, unconditionally running Bed.findByIdAndUpdate(data.bedId, {$set:{status:"Available", currentAdmission:null}}). :142-144 sets useTx only when session.client?.s?.options?.replicaSet is present, so a standalone Mongo (per-hospital Docker install) always takes this path. Controller passes req.body straight through (admissionController.js:21-22); route requires only ipd.assign-bed (admissionRoutes.js:93), so bedId is caller-controlled. REACHABILITY IS WORSE THAN CLAIMED (no race required). The per-patient 409 guard at :88-133 throws BEFORE run() is invoked, so it cannot trigger the rollback. Any single request with an already-Occupied bedId (stale bed list, back-button resubmit) deterministically frees another patient's bed on the first try. NO COMPENSATING GUARD. The unique index at admissionModel.js:536-539 is {UHID:1,status:1} — one Active admission per PATIENT, not per bed; nothing blocks two Active admissions on the same bedId. bedService.js:420 filters getAvailableBeds on status:"Available" only (housekeeping untouched by the rollback), so the wrongly-freed bed reappears in the picker…

findings.dimension: Concurrency & data integrity — races, TOCTOU, counters
findings.title: idempotencyGuard is check-then-act with no reservation — concurrent same-key POSTs BOTH execute the handler, double-posting CASH payments
findings.line: 84-135 (lookup at 85; cache write at 118 inside the patched res.json)
findings.mechanism: The guard reads `IdempotencyLog.findOne({key})` (L85) and, on a miss, calls `next()` (L135). The cache row is only created inside the monkey-patched `res.json` (L112-133) — i.e. AFTER the route handler has finished and already committed its side effects. Nothing is reserved between the lookup and the handler's completion, so the window between L85 and L118 is the entire handler duration (a multi-step bill load-modify-save). Two in-flight requests bearing the same Idempotency-Key both miss at L85 and both run the handler. The `unique:true` index on `key` (IdempotencyLogModel.js:26-33) only dedups the *cache row* — the L125 `.catch()` swallows the E11000 with the comment 'we don't care which side wins' (L116), but by then both handlers have already executed. The middleware is wired to every money endpoint: recordPayment, refundPayment, bulkCollectByUHID, bulkSettleByUHID, createAdvance, applyAdvanceToBill, refundAdvance (billingRoutes.js:112,116,168,185,240,245,250).
findings.failureScenario: Cashier double-clicks [Record Payment] for ₹5,000 CASH; the client sends two POSTs ~120ms apart with the same Idempotency-Key. Both hit L85 before either handler completes → both miss the cache → both enter recordPayment. The stated second line of defense does not catch it: the duplicate-transactionId guard at billingService.js:1658 is explicitly gated on `paymentMode !== "CASH"` AND a supplied transactionId, so CASH is exempt by design. The PatientBill optimisticConcurrency retry (L1644) does not deduplicate either — it merely serialises the two writers, so the second retry cleanly appends a SECOND ₹5,000 payment row. Bill is credited ₹10,000 against a ₹5,000 collection, flips to OVERPAID, and the shift cash-drawer reconciliation is short ₹5,000. The middleware's own header comment ('a cashier double-click or a network retry never posts two payment rows') is unmet under exactly the concurrency it names.
findings.fixSketch: Reserve before dispatch, not after. Insert a claim row atomically ahead of next(): `await IdempotencyLog.create({key, scope, requestHash, statusCode:null, responseBody:null})`; on E11000 the key is already claimed — re-read it and either replay the cached response (if responseBody is populated) or return 409 {code:"IDEMPOTENT_IN_FLIGHT"} so the client retries. Then have the patched res.json `updateOne({key},{ $set:{responseBody, statusCode} })` to complete the row. Also drop the in-flight claim if the handler throws, so a 500 does not permanently poison the key.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM FULLY CONFIRMED — every cited line matches verbatim. idempotencyGuard.js:85 does `await IdempotencyLog.findOne({key: trimmedKey}).lean()` and on a miss falls through to `next()` at L135 with NOTHING reserved. The cache row is created only inside the monkey-patched res.json (L111-133, `IdempotencyLog.create(...)` at L118) — i.e. after the handler has already committed its side effects — so the race window between L85 and L118 is the entire handler duration. L125-129 swallows E11000 ("Race — another writer already cached this key. Fine."), confirming the unique index (IdempotencyLogModel.js:26-33, `key: {type:String, required:true, unique:true}`) dedups only the CACHE ROW, not handler execution. Route wiring matches exactly: billingRoutes.js:112,116,168,185,240,245,250 (plus pharmacyRoutes.js:109,114). The claimed second line of defense is correctly characterised: billingService.js:1658 reads literally `if (paymentMode !== "CASH" && transactionId && String(transactionId).trim())`, with the comment at L1655-57 conceding "CASH has no canonical txn id so the guard skips it" — CASH is exempt by design. The MAX_RETRIES=5 loop at L1642-44 is optimisticConcurrency serialisation only and performs no dedup. My refutation attempt via a unique receiptNumber index FAILED: PatientBillModel.js:178 is `receiptNumber: {type:String, trim:true, index:true}` — indexed, NOT unique — so a second CASH row would append cleanly. WHY DOWNGRADE (reachability, not mechanism): the failureScenar…

findings.dimension: Concurrency & data integrity — races, TOCTOU, counters
findings.title: R8 #38 fix not applied to its named sibling: generateOPDAdmissionNumber still seeds by LEXICOGRAPHIC sort under an auto-widening 2-digit pad
findings.mechanism: `generateOPDAdmissionNumber` emits `OPD-YY-NN` via `String(seq).padStart(2,"0")` (L98) — a pad that auto-widens past 99 (OPD-26-100). When the counter doc `opd-admission:YY` is absent it self-heals the seed with `Admission.findOne({admissionNumber:{$regex:`^${prefix}`}}).sort({admissionNumber:-1})` (L93-94) — a STRING sort. Lexicographically 'OPD-26-99' > 'OPD-26-100' > 'OPD-26-105', so the 'max' resolves to 99 once the series has widened. This is precisely the bug R8 #38 fixed in billingService.generateBillNumber, whose fix comment at billingService.js:56-61 asserts it now 'matches the OPDService/CreditNote/PatientAdvance guarded pattern' — but OPDService was never converted and does not match. (CreditNote.js:111 and PatientAdvanceModel.js:241 use the same lexicographic sort but are SAFE, because their pads are a fixed 6 digits with `slice(-6)`; only OPDService pairs the lexicographic sort with a widening pad.) admissionNumber carries `unique:true sparse:true` (admissionModel.js:378), so the collision is not silent — it is a hard E11000.
findings.failureScenario: A hospital is past 99 OPD admissions for the year (OPD-26-01 … OPD-26-137) and the counters collection is lost or partially restored (mongodump restore of patient collections without `counters`, or a counters-collection repair). The next walk-in registration finds no `opd-admission:26` doc → L93 lexicographic sort returns 'OPD-26-99' → `seed = 99` → nextSequence returns 100 → emits 'OPD-26-100', which already exists → E11000 duplicate key, OPD registration fails at the reception desk. Each retry burns one sequence, so reception eats ~38 consecutive hard failures before the counter walks past 137 and registrations resume.
findings.fixSketch: Port the R8 #38 numeric-max scan verbatim from billingService.js:62-72: fetch `Admission.find({admissionNumber:{$regex:`^${prefix}`}}).select({admissionNumber:1}).lean()` and reduce with `parseInt(String(r.admissionNumber).slice(prefix.length),10)` taking the numeric max, keeping the existing 'only when the counter doc is absent' hot-path guard.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM CONFIRMED verbatim. OPDService.js:93-94 seeds via `Admission.findOne({admissionNumber:{$regex:`^${prefix}`}}).sort({admissionNumber:-1}).lean()` — a binary-collation STRING sort — while L98 emits `${prefix}${String(seq).padStart(2,"0")}`, a pad that auto-widens past 99. Descending byte-compare at the first digit after `OPD-26-` gives '9'(0x39) > '1'(0x31), so 'OPD-26-99' outranks 'OPD-26-137' and L95 yields seed=99; nextSequence(key,99) returns 100 → 'OPD-26-100', already in use. admissionModel.js:378 is `{type:String, trim:true, unique:true, sparse:true}`, so it is a hard E11000, not a silent overwrite. SIBLING CONTRAST CONFIRMED. CreditNote.js:111 and PatientAdvanceModel.js:241 use the same lexicographic sort but are genuinely safe because their pads are fixed 6 digits with slice(-6) (CreditNote.js:113/116, PatientAdvanceModel.js:243/246) — at fixed width, lexicographic order == numeric order. OPDService is the ONLY site pairing a string sort with a widening pad. The false-claim charge also holds: billingService.js:56-61 asserts its R8 #38 numeric-max fix "matches the OPDService/CreditNote/PatientAdvance guarded pattern" — OPDService matches the *guard* (scan only when the counter doc is absent, L85-87) but NOT the numeric-max *seed*. The fixSketch (port billingService.js:62-72's reduce) is correct and applies cleanly. FAILURE SCENARIO REFUTED — this is the basis for the downgrade. The auditor claims "OPD registration fails at the reception desk" and "reception e…

findings.dimension: Concurrency & data integrity — races, TOCTOU, counters
findings.title: UHID / patientId counter self-heals from countDocuments(), which under-counts after any patient deletion and re-issues a live UHID
findings.mechanism: When the `uhid:global` counter doc is absent, `ensureSeed` (L241-245) seeds it with `Patient.countDocuments()` (L273-275). UHIDs are issued as a dense 1..N sequence (`UH${String(seq).padStart(2,"0")}`, L281), so countDocuments equals the true max ONLY if no patient row was ever removed — it is a cardinality, not a high-water mark, and it can only ever under-estimate. Any hard delete makes seed < max(issued). `UHID` is `unique:true sparse:true` (L8), so the under-seed surfaces as E11000 rather than a silent merge of two patients' records. The identical pattern backs `_generateAdmissionNumber` (services/Patient/admissionService.js:33-38, seed = `Admission.countDocuments()`), which is comparatively safer only by luck: that count spans OPD+IPD rows so it over-estimates the IPD max. Note wipePatientData.js:428 resets `seq:0` while retaining the counter doc, so the routine wipe path correctly bypasses this seed — the exposure is counter-doc loss, not the wipe.
findings.failureScenario: Hospital has registered UH01..UH50 and hard-deletes 10 duplicate/test registrations → countDocuments()=40, while UH50 is still live. The counters collection is then lost or restored without the `counters` namespace. Next registration: ensureSeed finds no `uhid:global` → seed=40 → nextSequence returns 41 → emits 'UH41', which is still on a live patient → E11000 at the registration desk. The pre-save hook has no retry (the catch at L283 just forwards the error), so reception sees 10 consecutive opaque duplicate-key 500s — each burning one sequence — before the counter climbs past 50.
findings.fixSketch: Seed from a numeric high-water mark, not a count: scan `Patient.find({UHID:{$regex:'^UH'}}).select({UHID:1}).lean()` and reduce to `max(parseInt(UHID.slice(2),10))` (mirroring the R8 #38 numeric-max reduce in billingService.js:62-72). Apply the same to the per-year `patientId:${prefix}:${year}` seed at L263-269, whose `countDocuments({registrationType})` additionally spans ALL years while the key is year-scoped — harmless for collisions (the year is in the prefix) but it makes each January's series jump to the lifetime patient count.
findings.verifyEvidence: CODE IS AS CITED, IMPACT IS NOT. Every line the auditor cites is verbatim accurate: ensureSeed (patientModel.js:241-245) returns null if the counter doc exists and otherwise runs seedFn; UHID seeds from Patient.countDocuments() (L273-275) and is issued as a dense UH${pad(seq,2)} (L281); UHID is unique+sparse (L8); the catch at L283 just forwards the error with no retry; admissionService.js:33-38 mirrors the pattern with Admission.countDocuments(). utils/counter.js:88-100 confirms the seed is only honoured on first insert ($setOnInsert) and thereafter ignored. REFUTED PREMISE — no hard-delete path exists. The failure scenario requires "hard-deletes 10 duplicate/test registrations". The only patient-delete route is patientRoutes.js:42 (requireAction("patient.delete")) -> patientController.deletePatient (L312) -> patientService.deletePatient (L417), which terminates at patientService.js:556-558: "// Finally: archive the patient. patient.isActive = false; await patient.save();". It is a SOFT delete — the controller's own success string is "Patient archived". Even the Admin-only ?force=true cascade (L474-554) merely cancels admissions/bills/advances and still lands on the same archive; it never removes the Patient row. patientModel.js has NO query middleware (grep for pre(/post( returns only the L248 pre("save"); isActive is a plain field at L166), so an unfiltered Patient.countDocuments() still counts archived rows. Soft deletes therefore cannot lower the seed below max(issued).…

findings.title: Day Book counts a single voided payment as cash-out TWICE — netCashFlow off by 2x the voided amount
findings.mechanism: voidPayment stamps voidedAt on the original +X row AND pushes a compensating -X row with paidAt=now and NO voidedAt (billingService.js:1878-1893). The Day Book facets then both fire on the same void: `billRefundsOut` matches `_paidInWindow && payments.voidedAt $exists:false && _amt < 0` — which the compensating -X row satisfies exactly, so it is booked as a real refund; `reversedPayments` separately matches `_voidedInWindow && _amt > 0` — the original row — and is also added to cashOut (line 453). Meanwhile the original is excluded from `collections` by the `voidedAt $exists:false` filter (line 104), so nothing was ever booked into cashIn to offset either leg. True net cash movement of a same-day void is 0; the report yields -2X.
findings.failureScenario: Cashier collects Rs 5,000 CASH, notices a wrong-patient entry, and voids it 5 minutes later (same day). Rows: {+5000, paidAt=today, voidedAt=today} and {-5000, paidAt=today}. Day Book: collections=0, billRefundsOut=5000 (the compensating row), reversedPayments=5000 (the original) -> cashIn=0, cashOut=10000, netCashFlow=-Rs 10,000. The physical drawer is unchanged (money in, money back out = 0), so EOD tally shows a phantom Rs 10,000 shortfall and the cashier is held liable for cash that never left.
findings.fixSketch: Pick one accounting model, not both. Simplest and consistent with recalcTotals (PatientBillModel.js:684): drop the `voidedAt $exists:false` filters entirely and let the compensating row do the netting — collections sums all positive in-window rows, billRefundsOut sums all negative in-window rows, and delete the `reversedPayments` cashOut leg. Alternatively, tag the synthetic row (e.g. `isVoidReversal:true`) at billingService.js:1878 and exclude it from `billRefundsOut`. Add an E2E case asserting netCashFlow==0 for a same-day collect+void.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM FULLY CONFIRMED at every cited line. 1. Void writer (Backend/services/Billing/billingService.js:1873-1893): sets `pay.voidedAt = new Date()` on the ORIGINAL positive row, then UNCONDITIONALLY pushes a counter-row `{ amount: -Number(pay.amount), paidAt: new Date(), transactionId: "VOID-...", voidedById, voidedByRole }`. The pushed row sets voidedById/voidedByRole but NOT voidedAt. Backend/models/PatientBillModel/PatientBillModel.js:198 declares `voidedAt: { type: Date }` with NO default -> field is absent on the counter-row -> `$exists:false` matches it. 2. billRefundsOut facet (dayBookService.js:119-131): `_paidInWindow:true` (counter-row paidAt=now) AND `"payments.voidedAt": {$exists:false}` (absent) AND `_amt < 0` -> the SYNTHETIC reversal row is booked as a real refund = X. 3. reversedPayments facet (dayBookService.js:150-161): `_voidedInWindow:true` AND `_amt > 0` AND mode != ADVANCE_ADJUSTMENT -> the ORIGINAL row is booked again = X. 4. collections facet (dayBookService.js:100-109) excludes the original via `"payments.voidedAt": {$exists:false}`, so nothing is ever booked into cashIn to offset either leg. reversedRefunds (line 137-141) requires `_voidedInWindow && _amt < 0` — the counter-row has no voidedAt, so it does NOT offset. 5. Line 453: `cashOut = billRefundsOut + advanceRefundsOut + reversedPayments + pharmacyReturnsCash` -> both legs added. Line 454 netCashFlow = cashIn - cashOut - tds. Same-day collect Rs5000 + void => cashIn=0, cashOut=10000, netCas…

findings.title: AR aging subtracts a voided payment instead of ignoring it — fully-paid bills reappear as outstanding
findings.mechanism: The `_paid` filter excludes rows carrying voidedAt (the original +X) but happily includes the compensating -X row, which has no voidedAt. So a void contributes -X to _paid rather than 0, and `_outstanding = _gross - _paid` (line 946) is inflated by X. This directly contradicts the canonical balance at PatientBillModel.js:684, which sums every row so +X and -X cancel. Any bill whose payment was voided and re-collected therefore reports outstanding on a bill the system itself marks PAID.
findings.failureScenario: Bill of Rs 10,000. Patient pays Rs 10,000 by UPI; cashier typo'd the UTR and voids it, then re-collects Rs 10,000. Rows: {+10000, voidedAt set}, {-10000}, {+10000}. AR aging computes _paid = -10000 + 10000 = 0, _outstanding = Rs 10,000 (> 0.99 threshold at line 951), and the bill lands in the 0-30 patient bucket. bill.balanceAmount is 0 / billStatus PAID. Collections staff chase a patient who owes nothing, and the AR total handed to management overstates receivables by the full re-collected amount.
findings.fixSketch: Remove the `$not: ["$$p.voidedAt"]` predicate at line 934 and keep only the `paidAt <= asOf` predicate, so the compensating row nets the original (matching recalcTotals). If the voidedAt filter must stay, also exclude the synthetic reversal (needs the `isVoidReversal` marker suggested above). Cross-check with a test asserting AR aging total == sum(bill.balanceAmount) after a void+recollect.
findings.verifyEvidence: Every element of the finding is verified in code; refutation attempts failed on all three fronts. 1) VOID REALLY PUSHES A COMPENSATING ROW — billingService.js:1873-1893 (voidPayment): sets `pay.voidedAt = new Date()` on the ORIGINAL row (1873), then `b.payments.push({ amount: -Number(pay.amount), ..., paidAt: new Date(), remarks: 'VOID of payment ...' })` (1878-1893). The pushed reversal row sets receivedById/receivedByRole/voidedById/voidedByRole but NEVER voidedAt. This two-row design is documented in PatientBillModel.js:193-198: "the original row stays in place (audit immutability) and a NEGATIVE payment row is pushed alongside it". 2) THE FILTER LETS THE REVERSAL THROUGH — PatientBillModel.js:198 declares `voidedAt: { type: Date }` with no default, so the field is MISSING on the reversal row. `$not: ["$$p.voidedAt"]` (dashboardsController.js:934) on a missing path evaluates true, so the reversal row PASSES while the original it cancels is excluded. (A `default: null` would not help: `$not: [null]` is also true.) The sibling predicate `$lte: ["$$p.paidAt", asOf]` (935) does not exclude it either — the reversal's paidAt is the void timestamp. 3) ASYMMETRY VS CANONICAL BALANCE IS EXACT — PatientBillModel.js:684 `const totalPaid = this.payments.reduce((s, p) => s + toNum(p.amount), 0)` sums EVERY row so +X and -X cancel; voidPayment recomputes identically at line 1894 and sets billStatus = "PAID" at 1909 when balance <= 0.005. AR aging is the only consumer applying a one-sid…

findings.title: TPA MIS realization counts voided TPA remittances as settled — realizationPct can exceed 100%
findings.mechanism: `_tpaPaid` sums payment rows where `paymentMode == "TPA_CLAIM" && amount > 0`. It never inspects voidedAt, so a voided TPA remittance is still counted; and because the compensating row is negative, the `amount > 0` guard filters the offsetting -X out. The void is thus counted with the wrong sign in both directions — settledAmt is overstated by the full voided amount, and realizationPct (settledAmt/approvedAmt) is inflated. This is a genuine gap, not the intentional R8 #34 design (which only excludes TPA_CLAIM from the *patient's* balance, and says nothing about ignoring voids).
findings.failureScenario: Insurer remits Rs 1,00,000 against an approved Rs 1,00,000 claim. Claims clerk enters the wrong UTR, voids the row, and re-enters it. Rows: {+100000 TPA_CLAIM, voidedAt set}, {-100000 TPA_CLAIM}, {+100000 TPA_CLAIM}. _tpaPaid = 100000 + 100000 = 200000 (both positive rows; the -100000 reversal skipped by the amount>0 guard). TPA MIS reports settledAmt Rs 2,00,000 against approvedAmt Rs 1,00,000 and realizationPct = 200%. The desk believes the insurer has over-settled, stops chasing, and the byTpa per-insurer realization league table is corrupted for reconciliation.
findings.fixSketch: Drop the `$gt: [amount, 0]` guard in the $reduce at line 769 so the negative reversal nets the original, giving the true settled figure. (Removing the guard alone fixes both signs — no voidedAt check needed.) Clamp realizationPct display at a sane ceiling and log when settledAmt > approvedAmt as a data-quality alert.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM FULLY CONFIRMED; SEVERITY OVERSTATED. 1) Cited code is exactly as described. dashboardsController.js:765-772 `_tpaPaid` $reduce sums only rows where `$$this.paymentMode == "TPA_CLAIM"` AND `$gt: [{$toDouble: "$$this.amount"}, 0]`. It never inspects voidedAt, and the amount>0 guard filters out the compensating negative. Consumed at :783 (overall.settledAmt) and :791 (byTpa.settledAmt); realizationPct computed at :837 and :842 with no clamp. 2) Void row-shape claim verified at source (not inferred). billingService.js voidPayment():1873-1893 sets `pay.voidedAt/voidedBy/voidReason` on the ORIGINAL row while leaving its positive `amount` intact, then pushes `{ amount: -Number(pay.amount), paymentMode: pay.paymentMode, transactionId: `VOID-${...}` }`. The reversal inherits paymentMode (so TPA_CLAIM) and carries NO voidedAt. PatientBillModel.js:194-201 documents this contract verbatim ("the original row stays in place (audit immutability) and a NEGATIVE payment row is pushed alongside it"). TPA_CLAIM is in ALLOWED_MODES (billingService.js:1629) and voidPayment has no mode restriction, so TPA rows are voidable. 3) Failure scenario survives the existing guards. billingController.js:2317-2323 (tpaSettle) rejects a duplicate by `paymentMode=="TPA_CLAIM" && transactionId==body.transactionId` and does NOT exclude voided rows -- so re-entering the SAME UTR would 409. The finding's scenario specifies a WRONG UTR voided then re-entered with a DIFFERENT correct UTR, which passes cl…

findings.title: todayRevenue books a voided payment as a refund — same-day voids drive revenue negative
findings.mechanism: The pipeline excludes rows with voidedAt (line 88), removing the original +X from `billPayments`, but the compensating -X row has no voidedAt and so passes the filter and is captured by the `billRefundsOut` leg (`_amt < 0` -> $abs, lines 108-110). Line 232 then computes `revenue = billPayments + advanceAdjustments + pharmacyRevenue - billRefundsOut`, subtracting X for a transaction whose revenue contribution is zero. The module docstring (line 39-41) explicitly assumes 'negative payment rows (refunds) net out' — but a void reversal is not a refund, and nothing distinguishes the two. The same defect exists at billingService.js:2471+2479 (getBillingSummary).
findings.failureScenario: Quiet OPD morning: the only bill payment is Rs 5,000, which the cashier voids as a wrong-patient entry. GET /api/reports/today-revenue returns billPayments=0, billRefundsOut=5000, revenue = -Rs 5,000. The management dashboard shows negative revenue for the day, and the amount is silently misclassified as a patient refund in the refunds line that finance reconciles against actual cash disbursed.
findings.fixSketch: Same root fix as dayBookService: either drop the `payments.voidedAt $exists:false` filter (line 88) and let +X/-X net inside billPayments, or mark the synthetic reversal at billingService.js:1878 and exclude it from the billRefundsOut leg. Apply identically to billingService.getBillingSummary:2465-2482.
findings.verifyEvidence: Mechanism verified line-by-line; every claimed link holds. 1) billingService.js:1873-1877 stamps voidedAt/voidedBy/voidedById/voidedByRole/voidReason on the ORIGINAL +X payment row. Lines 1878-1893 then push the compensating reversal row: `{ amount: -Number(pay.amount), paymentMode: pay.paymentMode, transactionId: `VOID-${...}`, receivedById, receivedByRole, voidedById, voidedByRole, paidAt: new Date(), remarks: "VOID of payment ..." }`. The reversal sets voidedById and voidedByRole but NOT voidedAt — exactly the gap the finding asserts. Model docstring PatientBillModel.js:193-197 confirms this is the designed shape ("the original row stays in place ... and a NEGATIVE payment row is pushed alongside it"). 2) incomeService.js:86-89 `$match { "payments.paidAt": {$gte:from,$lt:to}, "payments.voidedAt": {$exists:false} }` — removes the original +X (now has voidedAt) but the reversal row passes (no voidedAt field). 3) incomeService.js:108-110 `billRefundsOut: { $sum: { $cond: [{ $lt: ["$_amt", 0] }, { $abs: "$_amt" }, 0] } }` — captures the surviving -X as a patient refund. 4) incomeService.js:232 `const revenue = +(billPayments + advanceAdjustments + pharmacyRevenue - billRefundsOut).toFixed(2)` → 0 + 0 + 0 - 5000 = -5000. Failure scenario reproduces exactly as filed. 5) Reachable in prod: reportsRoutes.js:42 `router.get("/today-revenue", requireAction("reports.financial"), dash.getTodayRevenue)` → dashboardsController.js:94 `await incomeService.todayRevenue()`. Second site conf…

findings.title: Day Book 'reversed refunds' cash-back leg (headline A6-CRIT-6 fix) is unreachable dead code
findings.mechanism: The facet matches `_voidedInWindow && _amt < 0` — i.e. a NEGATIVE payment row that carries voidedAt. billingService.voidPayment is the only writer of payments[].voidedAt in the codebase (verified by grep across Backend/), and it hard-refuses negative rows at lines 1844-1847 ('Cannot void a reversal entry', code ALREADY_REVERSAL). No code path can therefore ever produce a negative row with voidedAt set, so this facet always returns [] and `reversedRefunds` is always 0. The module docstring (lines 4-5, 22-40) advertises this as the fix for a cashier ending the day with a hole in the till; the cited condition cannot occur, and the docstring's own hedge ('a counter-row *may* also be pushed') is stale — voidPayment always pushes one.
findings.failureScenario: No incorrect number is emitted (the leg contributes a constant 0), but the safety net the code claims to provide does not exist: an operator attempting to roll back a mistaken refund gets ALREADY_REVERSAL, and the Day Book has no reversed-refund path at all. Worse, the dead facet gives false assurance during audit review that the reversed-refund case is handled, masking finding #1 (which is the *live* void path, and is doubly wrong).
findings.fixSketch: Delete the reversedRefunds facet and its cashIn term (line 450), or — if reversing a refund is a real business need — implement it in voidPayment and keep the leg. Either way correct the docstring at lines 4-40 to describe the actual two-part void (voidedAt stamp + always-pushed counter-row), since that stale description is what led the reporting layer to double-count.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM FULLY CONFIRMED — the facet is provably unreachable dead code. (1) dayBookService.js:137-147 requires `_voidedInWindow: true` AND `_amt < 0` — a NEGATIVE payments[] row bearing voidedAt. (2) billingService.js:1873 (`pay.voidedAt = new Date()`) is the ONLY writer of payments[].voidedAt. Every other grep hit across Backend/ is a different model or a reader: admissionService.js:889 and scripts/R7hr-163_cleanup_ramesh_dupes.js:73,104 and autoBillingService.js:4353,4492 all write BillingTrigger.voidedAt; intakeOutputService.js:261 writes IntakeOutputEntry; the rest (cashierSessionController:170, hospitalRegisterController:110,139, refundsController:64-94, dashboardsController:934,1014, index.js:523,588, form16aGenerator:94, incomeService:88, billingService:1663,1993,2471) are read-only filters. (3) billingService.js:1844-1847 refuses `Number(pay.amount) <= 0` with code ALREADY_REVERSAL, so the sole writer can only stamp voidedAt on a POSITIVE row. (4) NOT NOTED BY THE AUDITOR, strengthens the finding: the synthetic counter-row pushed at billingService.js:1878-1893 sets voidedById/voidedByRole but NOT voidedAt — so even it cannot satisfy the facet. (5) Legacy data ruled out: `git merge-base --is-ancestor 4e012df4 5ea5bc3c` returns true — the ALREADY_REVERSAL guard (4e012df4, introduced together with voidPayment itself) predates the reversedRefunds facet (5ea5bc3c). No code version ever produced a matching document; the facet was dead from birth. Docstring critique also h…

findings.title: ER / Day-Care statutory registers silently truncate at 2000 rows with no pagination or total
findings.mechanism: Both handlers accept a window up to maxDays:366 (lines 1134, 1154) but cap results with a bare `.limit(2000)` and no skip/page parameter. `count: rows.length` (lines 1146, 1162) reports the TRUNCATED length, not the true match count, so the response is indistinguishable from a complete register. Sorting is ascending on arrivalAt/createdAt, so it is the most recent entries that are dropped. Both also bypass the sendOk envelope every other endpoint in this file uses, emitting raw res.json with `from`/`to` as Date objects — so a client cannot detect truncation from the envelope either.
findings.failureScenario: A hospital running ~10 ER attendances/day requests the annual register for an NABH audit: GET /api/reports/er-register?from=2025-04-01&to=2026-03-31. ~3,650 rows match; the response contains the first 2,000 (Apr-Nov) and reports count:2000. The printed 'statutory ER register' is missing roughly four months of the most recent attendances, with nothing in the payload indicating incompleteness — an incomplete bound register presented to an accreditation assessor as complete.
findings.fixSketch: Add page/limit query params with a $facet (rows + $count) mirroring getDailyCollection:1038-1071, return a `pagination` block and the true total, and route both through sendOk for envelope consistency. At minimum, return `truncated:true` plus the real countDocuments when the cap is hit.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM: fully confirmed, verbatim, all four sub-claims. 1. Window up to 366 days — dashboardsController.js:1134 and :1154 both call `parseHospitalDateRange(req.query.from, req.query.to, { defaultDays: 31, maxDays: 366 })`. utils/queryGuards.js:127-143 confirms the helper honours caller-supplied from/to and only rejects at >maxDays, so a 365-day window is accepted. 2. Bare cap, no skip/page — line 1144 `.limit(2000)` and line 1160 `.limit(2000)`. Neither handler reads `req.query.page`/`req.query.limit`; there is no `.skip()`. Contrast getDailyCollection:1005-1007 which does read page/limit and :1040-1047 which uses `$facet` with `total: [{ $count: "n" }]`, and :1071 which returns a real `pagination` block. So the fixSketch's cited model is real and this file already contains the correct pattern. 3. `count` reports the truncated length — line 1146 `res.json({ success: true, from, to, count: rows.length, data: rows })` and line 1162 identically. `rows.length` is post-`.limit(2000)`, so at the cap it is literally 2000 with no `countDocuments` anywhere in either handler. The payload is indistinguishable from a complete register. 4. Ascending sort drops the newest — line 1143 `.sort({ arrivalAt: 1 })`, line 1159 `.sort({ createdAt: 1 })`. Confirmed the dropped tail is the most recent entries. 5. Envelope bypass — both use raw `res.json` while every neighbouring endpoint uses `sendOk` (e.g. :1072-1074, :1117-1119). `from`/`to` are the Date objects returned by parseHospitalDateRa…

findings.title: TTL index on BillingAudit.retainUntil hard-deletes audit rows before the Sunday archiver can ever move them — cold storage stays permanently empty
findings.mechanism: BillingAuditSchema.index({ retainUntil: 1 }, { expireAfterSeconds: 0 }) makes MongoDB's TTL monitor permanently delete any BillingAudit doc once retainUntil <= now (reaper sweeps every ~60s). The `billing-audit-archive` cron that is supposed to MIGRATE those rows into the `billing_audit_archive` cold collection (index.js:757) (a) only proceeds on Sundays (index.js:760-763, `if (dow !== "Sun") return`), (b) selects exactly the already-doomed set `BillingAudit.find({ retainUntil: { $lt: now } })` (index.js:765), and (c) caps at 1000 rows. Since the TTL reaper removes each row within ~60s of expiry, the archiver's query can only ever match rows that expired in the <=60s window before it happens to run at 03:30 IST on a Sunday — in practice it always returns 0 and returns `{ moved: 0 }`. Nothing is ever copied to cold storage; the rows are destroyed. emitBillingAudit uses `create(row)` (BillingAudit.js:338), so the pre-save hook at 251-268 always stamps retainUntil, meaning every row is TTL-eligible. The code comment at index.js:751-753 asserts the opposite ("The archive collection is append-only too — never delete").
findings.failureScenario: A cashier records a payment → BILL_PAYMENT_RECORDED row created with retainUntil = now + 7y (_FINANCIAL_EVENTS, BillingAudit.js:191/247). Exactly 7 years later the MongoDB TTL monitor deletes that row from `billing_audits` within 60 seconds of expiry. The next Sunday 03:30 IST tick of `billing-audit-archive` queries `retainUntil < now`, finds nothing, and reports `{ moved: 0 }` as a healthy CRON_RECONCILED heartbeat. `billing_audit_archive` is empty. A GST Act §35 / IT Rule 46 assessment or an NABH IPSG.6 surveyor asking for the payment trail gets nothing — the row was destroyed, not archived, and no alert ever fired. Worse for the 1-year class: any event name not in _FINANCIAL_EVENTS/_ADMIN_EVENTS falls through _retainYearsFor() to `return 1` (BillingAudit.js:249) and is hard-deleted after 12 months.
findings.fixSketch: Drop the `expireAfterSeconds: 0` TTL index (or repoint it at a separate `purgeAfter` field that the archiver sets only AFTER a confirmed copy into billing_audit_archive). Give the archiver a lead time so it archives BEFORE expiry — e.g. select `retainUntil < now + 30d`, copy to archive, then set purgeAfter. Remove the Sunday-only gate and the 1000-row cap (or loop until drained), and assert archive count growth so a silent `moved: 0` cannot look healthy.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM CONFIRMED — every cited line reads as described. 1) TTL index is real and unconditional. D:\Spherehealth\Backend\models\Billing\BillingAudit.js:281 — `BillingAuditSchema.index({ retainUntil: 1 }, { expireAfterSeconds: 0 });`. No `partialFilterExpression`, unlike the sibling legal-hold-aware TTLs (GateLogModel.js:88, PatientActivityLogModel.js:127 pattern). The comment at 275-280 explicitly frames it as a reaper ("reaped within ~60s of expiry even when the archiver is down"). 2) Every row is TTL-eligible. Field defaults to now+7y (BillingAudit.js:176); pre-save hook (251-268) always stamps a concrete `retainUntil` on `isNew` via `_retainYearsFor()` (246-250). `emitBillingAudit` uses `mongoose.model("BillingAudit").create(row)` (line 338), so the save hook always runs. `retainUntil` is never null → never TTL-exempt. 3) The archiver's query is exactly the TTL-doomed set, so it is dead code. index.js:765 — `BillingAudit.find({ retainUntil: { $lt: now } }).sort({createdAt:1}).limit(1000)`, gated by `if (dow !== "Sun") return { skipped: "non-Sunday", moved: 0 }` (760-763) and firing at 03:30 IST (754). MongoDB's TTL monitor sweeps ~every 60s, so a week's worth of expiries is destroyed long before the Sunday tick; the query can only ever match rows that expired in the <=60s window straddling the run. `billing_audit_archive` (757) never populates, and `{ moved: 0 }` (767) is indistinguishable from healthy. The comment at index.js:751-753 ("The archive collection is append-…

findings.title: Retention startup self-test runs Model.find({}).limit(0) — MongoDB treats limit 0 as UNLIMITED, so every boot streams 12 entire collections into heap
findings.mechanism: startupSelfTest intends a no-op reachability probe: `await Model.find({}).limit(0).lean()`. Mongoose's Query.prototype.limit (node_modules/mongoose/lib/query.js:915-928) does no zero-special-casing — it simply assigns `this.options.limit = v`, so the driver issues find with `limit: 0`, which in the MongoDB wire protocol means NO LIMIT, not "zero documents". The probe therefore fetches and hydrates the FULL contents of all 12 KNOWN_MODELS collections — PatientBill, DoctorNotes, MAR, Prescription, Admission, DischargeSummary, ConsentForm, PharmacySale, ScheduleXEntry, PharmacyDrugBatch, PharmacyVendorReturn, MLCReport — on every process start. index.js:1136 fires this as an un-awaited IIFE at boot, so all 12 full-collection reads race concurrently.
findings.failureScenario: A hospital 3 years into production has ~400k PatientBill + ~1.2M MAR + ~800k DoctorNotes rows. On every container restart/redeploy, index.js:1147 calls startupSelfTest, which issues 12 concurrent unbounded find() calls. The node process allocates the entire result sets as lean objects, blows past the default V8 heap, and the container dies with JavaScript heap out of memory — then the orchestrator restarts it and it OOMs again, an unrecoverable crash-loop triggered purely by data growth, with no code change. Even short of OOM, boot stalls for minutes and hammers the DB while the app is already serving traffic.
findings.fixSketch: Use `.limit(1)` (or `.findOne().select('_id')`, or `Model.estimatedDocumentCount()`) — anything but limit(0). The probe only needs to prove the model is registered and the collection round-trips. Add `.maxTimeMS(5000)` too, and await the IIFE or make the check sequential so a slow probe cannot pile on at boot.
findings.verifyEvidence: The cited code exists verbatim and the mechanism holds end-to-end. 1. D:\Spherehealth\Backend\services\MRD\retentionEnforcer.js:233 — `try { await Model.find({}).limit(0).lean(); results.push({ name, ok: true }); }`, inside `startupSelfTest()` (line 211), looping over the 12 KNOWN_MODELS at lines 215-228 (DoctorNotes, MAR, ConsentForm, PatientBill, DischargeSummary, Prescription, PharmacySale, ScheduleXEntry, PharmacyDrugBatch, PharmacyVendorReturn, Admission, MLCReport). The JSDoc at line 206 explicitly calls it "a no-op `find().limit(0)`" — confirming the author's intent was a zero-document probe, i.e. this is a genuine mistake, not a deliberate scan. 2. Mongoose does no zero-special-casing. Backend/package.json:26 pins `"mongoose": "^8.17.1"`; node_modules/mongoose/lib/query.js:915-928 `Query.prototype.limit` casts strings then does a bare `this.options.limit = v;` — 0 is passed straight through. 3. The driver forwards it as `limit: 0`. node_modules/mongodb/lib/operations/find.js:71-79: `if (typeof options.limit === 'number') { if (options.limit < 0) {...} else { findCommand.limit = options.limit; } }` — 0 is a number and is not < 0, so `findCommand.limit = 0` is emitted. In the MongoDB find command, `limit: 0` is documented as equivalent to setting NO limit. So each probe issues an unbounded full-collection find and `.lean()` materializes the entire result array in heap. 4. No mitigation exists: a repo-wide grep for `mongoose.plugin(` / global `maxTimeMS` returns no matc…

findings.title: A single failed backup run overwrites last-backup.json with {ok:false}, which permanently disables the >50%-shrink guard on the next run — a gutted backup then archives and prunes
findings.line: 131-134 (shrink guard reads prev.ok), 196 (good status write), 202 (failure clobbers the SAME file), 34 (MIN_DOCS default 1)
findings.mechanism: The shrink guard is the only meaningful content check: `if (!ALLOW_SHRINK && prev && prev.ok && prev.totalDocs && res.totalDocs < prev.totalDocs * 0.5)`. Its baseline `prev` is read from `OFFLINE/last-backup.json` (line 125). But the catch block at line 202 writes `{ ok: false, error, at }` to that exact same path on ANY failure, destroying the last-known-good totalDocs baseline. On the following run `prev.ok` is false, so the `prev && prev.ok && prev.totalDocs` conjunction short-circuits and the shrink check is skipped entirely. The only remaining floor is `res.totalDocs < MIN_DOCS` with MIN_DOCS defaulting to `Math.max(0, Number(process.env.BACKUP_MIN_DOCS) || 1)` = 1 doc and MIN_COLLS = 1 collection (lines 34-35) — which almost any broken backup clears. The run then proceeds to archive (line 138), write last-backup.json with ok:true (line 196) — re-baselining the ratchet DOWN — and prune old good copies (line 191-192).
findings.failureScenario: Night 1: the Mongo connection blips mid-backup; run() throws, line 202 writes {ok:false} into last-backup.json, wiping the 4,000,000-doc baseline. Night 2: a mis-scoped credential means the driver can only read 3 of 180 collections, so backupDatabase returns totalDocs=12,000. MIN_DOCS(1)/MIN_COLLS(1) pass. The shrink guard is skipped because prev.ok===false. The 12k-doc backup is renamed into place, validates (it is internally consistent, footer and per-collection counts match — validateBackupFile at line 141 only checks self-consistency, never breadth), prune() runs, and last-backup.json now records ok:true totalDocs=12000 as the new baseline. Every subsequent 12k-doc backup now passes the shrink guard against the poisoned 12k baseline. Fourteen nights later prune(offlineDir, NIGHTLY_KEEP=14) has deleted the last real 4M-doc archive. The hospital's only backups are 0.3% of the database, and last-backup.json says ok:true.
findings.fixSketch: Keep the last-GOOD status in a separate file (e.g. last-good-backup.json) that the failure path never touches, and read the shrink baseline from that; write failures to last-backup-error.json (or add the error alongside without clobbering totalDocs). Additionally, raise the default MIN_COLLS to the live DB's actual collection count (compare `res.collections.length` against `listCollections` on MONGO_URI) so a partial-read backup can never clear the floor.
findings.verifyEvidence: All cited lines are exact and the mechanism is real in Backend/scripts/backup/runBackup.js. Line 125 reads the shrink baseline from OFFLINE/last-backup.json (`const prev = readJson(path.join(OFFLINE, "last-backup.json"))`). Line 131 gates the only content-breadth check on that baseline: `if (!ALLOW_SHRINK && prev && prev.ok && prev.totalDocs && res.totalDocs < prev.totalDocs * 0.5)` — the conjunction short-circuits when prev.ok is false, skipping the check entirely. Line 196 writes the good status {ok:true,...,totalDocs} to that same path, and line 202 (the catch) writes {ok:false,error,at} to the SAME path on ANY failure, with no totalDocs — destroying the last-known-good baseline. Lines 34-35 confirm MIN_DOCS = Math.max(0, Number(process.env.BACKUP_MIN_DOCS)||1) = 1 and MIN_COLLS = 1, so the remaining floor at line 127 is 1 doc / 1 collection. No mitigation exists: a repo-wide grep for `last-good`/`last-backup.json` finds only this writer plus readers (Backend/services/backup/backupAdminService.js:59,101; deploy/provision-hospital.sh:102) — there is no separate last-good file. validateBackupFile at line 141 only checks self-consistency (checksum/footer/per-collection counts), never breadth, so a gutted-but-internally-consistent backup validates; prune() at 191-192 then runs. The finding actually UNDERSTATES the defect: the shrink-guard throw at line 133 and the content-floor throw at line 129 both route through the same catch at line 202, so the guard destroys its own base…

findings.title: Docker/Linux deployments get nightly-only, offline-only backups on the same host as the DB — the monthly archive and the restore-drill exist only in a Windows-only PowerShell task
findings.line: 1328-1331 (only scheduleDaily backup registration); Backend/scripts/backup/runBackup.js:169-188 (drill gated on isMonthly/--verify), 41 (MONTHLY_KEEP), 191; Backend/scripts/backup/setup-backup-tasks.ps1:49-59; docker-compose.yml:55-65
findings.mechanism: index.js registers exactly ONE backup job: `scheduleDaily("nightly-mongo-backup", 2, 30, () => run({ mode: "nightly" }))`. There is no `mode: "monthly"` and no `verify: true` anywhere in the in-process scheduler (grep for `monthly` in index.js returns only gst-monthly-snapshot and antibiogram-monthly-rollup). runBackup.js gates the real restore-drill on `if (isMonthly || (opts.verify != null ? !!opts.verify : flag("verify")))` (line 169) — so in-process it NEVER runs; the only verification is validateBackupFile (line 141), which re-reads the file's own footer and per-collection counts and can never detect that the archive is unrestorable into a live Mongo. The monthly path + drill are registered only by setup-backup-tasks.ps1 (lines 49-59), a Windows Task Scheduler script that cannot run in the node:alpine container. Meanwhile docker-compose.yml:61 sets `BACKUP_ALLOW_OFFLINE_ONLY: "${BACKUP_ALLOW_OFFLINE_ONLY:-1}"` — defaulting the documented "OFF-SITE IS MANDATORY" control (runBackup.js:12-13, 161-165) to OFF — and BACKUP_OFFLINE_DIR: /app/backups maps to the `backend-backups` Docker volume on the same host as the Mongo data volume. MONTHLY_KEEP=24 (line 41) is dead config on these deploys.
findings.failureScenario: A hospital is provisioned via deploy/provision-hospital.sh with compose defaults. Every night at 02:30 IST the in-process cron writes sphere_YYYYMMDD_HHMM.shbak.gz into the backend-backups volume; prune() keeps NIGHTLY_KEEP=14, so retention is 14 days — against an NABH IMS.3 / GST §35 floor of 5-8 years that retentionEnforcer.js:30-34 itself encodes. No sphere_YYYYMM.shbak.gz is ever produced. No restore is ever attempted, so a systematic restore defect (e.g. an index-rebuild or EJSON round-trip failure) sits undetected indefinitely. On day 400 the host's disk array fails: the Mongo volume and the backend-backups volume die together, BACKUP_ALLOW_OFFLINE_ONLY=1 means no off-site copy was ever required or made, and the hospital loses the entire HIS with zero recoverable archives — while last-backup.json had reported ok:true every single night.
findings.fixSketch: Register a second in-process job that runs run({ mode: 'monthly' }) on the 1st IST (guard inside the fn on the IST day-of-month, the way billing-audit-archive gates on Sunday at index.js:760-763), so the monthly archive + restore-drill actually execute on Linux/Docker. Flip the compose default to BACKUP_ALLOW_OFFLINE_ONLY=0 and make provision-hospital.sh require an off-host BACKUP_SYNCED_DIR (bind-mounted remote/object-store path). Fail loudly at boot if BACKUP_OFFLINE_DIR resolves onto the same device as the Mongo data volume.
findings.verifyEvidence: CORE MECHANISM CONFIRMED — every cited line is accurate: 1. Backend/index.js:1328-1331 registers exactly one backup job: `scheduleDaily("nightly-mongo-backup", 2, 30, async () => { const { run } = require("./scripts/backup/runBackup"); return await run({ mode: "nightly" }); })`. A case-insensitive grep for `monthly|runBackup|verify:` across index.js returns only gst-monthly-snapshot (line 313), antibiogram-monthly-rollup (line 1084), and the single require at 1329. There is NO `mode:"monthly"` and NO `verify:true` anywhere in the in-process scheduler. 2. runBackup.js:106 `const isMonthly = mode === "monthly"` and :169 `if (isMonthly || (opts.verify != null ? !!opts.verify : flag("verify")))` — the in-process caller passes neither, and `flag("verify")` reads process.argv of the SERVER process, so the restore-drill block (169-188) never executes on the scheduled path. Confirmed: MONTHLY_KEEP (line 41) and the `sphere_YYYYMM.shbak.gz` naming (line 108) are unreachable from any Linux/Docker scheduler; the `monthly/` subdir (line 107) is never created. 3. The monthly path exists ONLY in setup-backup-tasks.ps1:49-59 (`New-ScheduledTaskAction ... --mode=monthly`, MSFT_TaskMonthlyTrigger DaysOfMonth=1), a Windows Task Scheduler registration that cannot run in node:alpine. Confirmed. 4. docker-compose.yml:55-65 confirmed: BACKUP_OFFLINE_DIR /app/backups → `backend-backups` volume, alongside the mongo volume; `BACKUP_ALLOW_OFFLINE_ONLY: "${BACKUP_ALLOW_OFFLINE_ONLY:-1}"` defaults the…

findings.title: missedDoseCron hydrates every DoctorOrder that ever had an AR row — including Completed ones, with no date bound — every 15 minutes, and its 10-min lock TTL is shorter than the sweep can take
findings.line: 77-80 (unbounded find), 82-145 (full hydration loop), 161 (10-min lock TTL vs 15-min interval)
findings.mechanism: `DoctorOrder.find({ status: { $in: ['Pending','Acknowledged','Active','InProgress','Completed','OnHold'] }, 'administrationRecord.0': { $exists: true } })` has no date predicate, no .limit(), no .lean() (full mongoose hydration is required because the code mutates subdocs and calls order.save()). "Completed" is deliberately included (comment lines 69-76), and a Completed order never leaves the result set — so the working set is every medication order the hospital has ever written, re-fetched and re-walked every 15 minutes forever, even though every AR row in those old orders is already terminal and the loop flips nothing. Separately, arm() takes `acquireLock('cron:missed-dose', 10 * 60)` (line 161) on a 15-minute interval (index.js:1253); when the sweep exceeds the 10-minute TTL, cronScheduler.acquireLock's `expiresAt: { $lt: now }` predicate (cronScheduler.js:73) lets the NEXT tick rotate the holder and start a concurrent sweep over the same orders.
findings.failureScenario: Two years in, a hospital has ~250k DoctorOrders with administrationRecord entries, almost all Completed. At every 15-minute tick the cron pulls all 250k as hydrated Mongoose documents (each with a full AR array), spiking heap by hundreds of MB and saturating the Mongo connection pool for minutes — 96 times a day — while flipping zero rows. Once a tick crosses 10 minutes, the lock TTL expires mid-sweep, the next tick acquires it (cronScheduler.js:73 matches the expired doc), and two sweeps walk the same orders concurrently; both mutate row.status and push auditLog entries and both call order.save(), producing duplicated 'MAR slot auto-missed' auditLog rows and duplicate MAR_DOSE_MISSED ClinicalAudit emissions on the same slot. Then the first sweep's releaseLock (line 164) is a no-op (holder no longer matches), so nothing is corrupted further — but the register now has duplicate NABH MOM.4 entries.
findings.fixSketch: Bound the query: add `updatedAt: { $gte: new Date(Date.now() - 7*86400000) }` (or scope on the earliest pending scheduledDate) plus a `.limit()` with cursor paging, and drop 'Completed' from the $in unless the order also has a pending AR row past cutoff (add an index on administrationRecord.status + scheduledDate and match on it server-side). Raise the lock TTL above the worst-case sweep duration (or renew it mid-sweep) so it can never expire while the tick is running.
findings.verifyEvidence: All three cited mechanisms verified in the actual code; I attempted to refute each and could not. 1) UNBOUNDED SCAN — CONFIRMED. `D:\Spherehealth\Backend\services\Clinical\missedDoseCron.js:77-80` is verbatim:    DoctorOrder.find({ status: { $in: ["Pending","Acknowledged","Active","InProgress","Completed","OnHold"] }, "administrationRecord.0": { $exists: true } });    No date predicate, no .limit(), no .lean(), no cursor. Full Mongoose hydration is genuinely required — the loop mutates `row.status`/`row.notes` in place (lines 98-101), pushes to `order.auditLog` (103-108) and calls `order.save()` (134), so .lean() is not an available shortcut. "Completed" is deliberate per the R7bq-K comment (69-76), and because a Completed order's AR rows are already terminal, it matches the predicate forever and flips nothing — the working set is monotonically growing and never self-prunes. Index check on `D:\Spherehealth\Backend\models\Doctor\DoctorOrderModel.js:721-726` shows only {sourceRef}, {UHID,status}, {visitId,status}, {UHID,orderType}, {admissionId,status}, {ipdNo,orderType,status} — none has `status` or `administrationRecord` as a usable prefix, so this is an unindexed COLLSCAN + full hydration of the entire medication-order history, 96×/day. Caller confirmed at `D:\Spherehealth\Backend\index.js:1251-1254`: arm({ intervalMs: 15 * 60 * 1000 }). 2) LOCK TTL < WORST-CASE SWEEP — CONFIRMED. `missedDoseCron.js:161` is `acquireLock('cron:missed-dose', 10 * 60)` on a 15-min interval, wi…

findings.title: scheduleDaily honours HOSPITAL_TZ for the day-key and log line but hardcodes a +05:30 offset when computing the actual fire instant
findings.mechanism: TZ is configurable (`process.env.HOSPITAL_TZ || 'Asia/Kolkata'`) and both DAY_KEY_FMT and HOUR_FMT are built with `timeZone: TZ`. But nextRunAt() constructs the target instant as `new Date(`${istKey}T${HH}:${MM}:00+05:30`)` — a literal IST offset that ignores TZ entirely. So the day-key is computed in the operator's configured zone while the clock time is anchored to India. The same hardcoding is acknowledged elsewhere (missedDoseCron.js:36-42 routes through queryGuards.istStartOfToday, which is also IST-fixed), so the whole daily-cron fleet silently assumes IST regardless of HOSPITAL_TZ, while the boot log at line 228 prints `HOUR_FMT.format(at)` in TZ and therefore reports the configured-zone rendering of an IST instant — making the misfire invisible in logs.
findings.failureScenario: An operator deploys for a Gulf hospital and sets HOSPITAL_TZ=Asia/Dubai (UTC+4). They expect eod-day-book (index.js:506, 23:55) to close the day book at 23:55 Dubai time. nextRunAt builds the Dubai day-key but appends +05:30, so the job actually fires at 22:25 Dubai time — 90 minutes BEFORE the local day ends. Every transaction booked between 22:25 and midnight local lands after the day-book snapshot and is either missing from that day's close or double-counted the next day. shift-auto-close (23:50) misfires the same way, closing shifts 90 minutes early. Nothing in the logs shows it: line 228 renders the (wrong) instant through the Dubai formatter and prints '22:25 IST'-labelled Dubai text that an operator reads as correct-looking output.
findings.fixSketch: Compute the offset for TZ at the target date instead of hardcoding it — e.g. derive the zone offset via Intl.DateTimeFormat with timeZoneName:'longOffset' (or an `Intl` two-pass: format the candidate instant in TZ, diff against the desired wall-clock, and correct) — then build the Date from that. Alternatively adopt a TZ-aware library (luxon `DateTime.fromObject({...}, { zone: TZ })`). If IST-only is the intent, delete HOSPITAL_TZ and hardcode 'Asia/Kolkata' everywhere so the config cannot lie.
findings.verifyEvidence: MECHANISM CONFIRMED VERBATIM. cronScheduler.js:25 `const TZ = process.env.HOSPITAL_TZ || "Asia/Kolkata"`; :92-97 DAY_KEY_FMT + HOUR_FMT both use `timeZone: TZ`; :106 `new Date(`${istKey}T${HH}:${MM}:00+05:30`)` hardcodes IST and never reads TZ. The comment at :102-104 ("Works regardless of host TZ because we anchor on the IST calendar") confirms the IST anchor is deliberate but is not flagged as a limit on the knob. HOSPITAL_TZ is genuinely advertised as configurable (deploy/.env.example:25 "IANA timezone for IST-anchored crons"; docker-compose.yml:51; autoBillingService.js:175 "Override with HOSPITAL_TZ in .env if deployed outside India"), so the config can lie. Arithmetic checks out: with TZ=Asia/Dubai, scheduleDaily("eod-day-book",23,55) builds Dubai day-key + "+05:30" = 18:25 UTC = 22:25 Dubai, 90 min before intended. IMPACT REFUTED ON FOUR COUNTS -> severity medium is too high. (1) No missing/double-counted revenue. index.js:516-517 anchors the aggregation window with the SAME hardcoded +05:30 (`new Date(`${dayStr}T00:00:00+05:30`)` .. +86400000). Fire instant and query window are both IST-anchored and therefore mutually consistent: payments in the 22:25-midnight Dubai gap fall inside the NEXT run's window and are counted exactly once. The real defect is local-day misattribution, not a reconciliation gap or double-count. (2) shift-auto-close does not misfire as claimed. index.js:568-575 selects `status:"OPEN", openedAt < now-16h` over a `[s.openedAt, new Date()]` window…

findings.dimension: Frontend — state, API wiring, XSS, secrets, print/render
findings.title: Stored XSS: unescaped Morse/GCS/MEWS score values interpolated into note-card HTML injected via dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.mechanism: printNurseNote.js builds raw HTML strings and escapes almost every interpolation via the shared escapeHtml() (e.g. the Braden table at :358 does `escapeHtml(fmtVal(r[1]))`). Three sibling renderers omit the escaper on the value column: - :399 (Morse fall) `${fmtVal(r[1]) || "—"}` and `${totalDisplay != null ? totalDisplay : "—"}` where `totalDisplay = f.total ?? ...` - :434 (GCS) `${fmtVal(g[1]) || "—"}` and `${gcsTotal ?? "—"}` - :473 (MEWS) `${fmtVal(r[1]) || "—"}` and `${m.total ?? "—"}` fmtVal() (:33-66) returns strings verbatim — it does no escaping. The values come straight from note data. That data is fully attacker-controlled server-side: nurseNotesService.js:59-61 sweeps EVERY non-BASE_FIELDS key of the request body into `noteData` with zero validation ("nothing is lost"), and NurseNotesModel.js:178 types noteData as `Schema.Types.Mixed`, so no inner key is schema-checked. The resulting string is injected unsanitized at three React sinks: PatientPanelTabs.jsx:97 (NoteCardEmbed), TimelineNoteCard.jsx:279, and NursingNotes.jsx:2562. The UI restricts these fields to buttons/selects, but the UI is not the trust boundary — the API is.
findings.failureScenario: A nurse (or any account able to POST a nursing note — the lowest clinical privilege) sends `POST /api/nurse-notes` with body `{ patientUHID:"UH00000001", noteType:"fall", fallRisk:{ total:"" } }`. `fallRisk` is not in BASE_FIELDS, so it is stored verbatim into noteData (Mixed). When ANY user — including an admin or the treating consultant — opens that patient's panel (PatientPanelTabs), the doctor timeline (TimelineNoteCard), or the nursing notes list, buildNurseNoteCardHtml emits the payload unescaped at :399, React injects it via dangerouslySetInnerHTML, and the script executes in the viewer's authenticated origin. Since the JWT lives in sessionStorage under `his_token` (config/axiosInterceptor.js:39-55) and is readable by same-tab script, the attacker exfiltrates an admin session token — privilege escalation from nurse to admin, plus arbitrary authenticated API calls (mass PHI read) as the victim. The payload also re-executes in every print/Complete-File render of that note.
findings.fixSketch: Wrap all three value columns and the three totals in the escaper, matching the Braden row at :358 — `escapeHtml(fmtVal(r[1]))`, `escapeHtml(String(totalDisplay))`, `escapeHtml(String(gcsTotal))`, `escapeHtml(String(m.total))`. Grep the builders for any `${` inside a template literal that is not wrapped by escapeHtml/_kv/_grid to catch further gaps. Defence in depth: validate/coerce numeric score fields (m1..m6, gcse/gcsv/gcsm, rr/hr/spo2, total) to Number server-side in nurseNotesService before they reach the Mixed bucket, and consider a DOMPurify pass on builder output at the three dangerouslySetInnerHTML sinks.
findings.verifyEvidence: All three cited sinks verified verbatim in Frontend/src/pages/nursing/printNurseNote.js. :399 emits `${fmtVal(r[1]) || "—"}` and `${totalDisplay != null ? totalDisplay : "—"}`; :434 emits `${fmtVal(g[1]) || "—"}` and `${gcsTotal ?? "—"}`; :473 emits `${fmtVal(r[1]) || "—"}` and `${m.total ?? "—"}` — none wrapped in escapeHtml. The sibling Braden renderer at :358 in the same file does it correctly (`escapeHtml(fmtVal(r[1]))`, `escapeHtml(String(total))`), and within the very same template literals at :399/:473 the LABEL column IS wrapped (`escapeHtml(r[0])`) while the VALUE column is not — proving omission, not an intentional raw-HTML slot. fmtVal (:33-66) does no escaping (line 44 `return v;` returns strings verbatim; :61 recursively concatenates object values). Source confirmed: nurseNotesService.js:59-63 sweeps every key not in BASE_FIELDS (:22-32 — fallRisk/neuroAssessment/mewsScore all absent) into noteData with no validation; NurseNotesModel.js:178 is `mongoose.Schema.Types.Mixed`, so no inner key is schema-checked. printNurseNote.js:141/:154 (`const nd = note.noteData || note.noteDetails || {}`, `get = (key) => nd[key] || topLvl[key] || {}`) reads it straight back. No mitigating control at any layer: Backend/index.js:105 sets `contentSecurityPolicy: false` in helmet (so an injected onerror handler is NOT blocked); no body sanitizer (xss/mongo-sanitize/stripHtml) in index.js; grep for DOMPurif…

findings.dimension: Frontend — state, API wiring, XSS, secrets, print/render
findings.title: Logout PHI sweep misses every clinical draft — `sphere_draft_*` autosave PHI survives logout on shared terminals
findings.mechanism: The logout() sweep enumerates local+sessionStorage and removes keys matching a hardcoded prefix list: `["nabh_", "his_patient_", "his_admission_", "rc_", "sphereai_"]` (plus `break-glass:`). Its own comment states the security intent ("the next user on a shared terminal can't open DevTools and harvest the previous user's patient context. Security audit 2026-05-17 finding E-01 / E-02") and warns "Keep this list in sync with whatever pages call localStorage.setItem with a patient-scoped key." That list has drifted out of sync.
